title: Family dynamics are a missing piece in understanding residential solar adoption - Nature Energy
gist: Two US studies find that residential solar adoption is not an individual decision but a household one: 'in sync' couples adopt while 'oppositional' ones do not, external catalysts like expiring tax credits or rising bills trigger action, and greater whole-household involvement - even disagreement - predicts adoption.

insights.claim: Residential solar adoption is better modeled as a household decision shaped by intrahousehold dynamics than as the individual-level decision it is typically conceptualized as.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Reframes solar customer acquisition around households, not individuals - a marketing/targeting insight for residential solar installers and lead-gen firms.

insights.claim: Interviews with 39 couples identified 'in sync' role sets (shared goals and division of labor) among adopter households and 'oppositional' role sets (disagreement) among non-adopters.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Household alignment is a screenable adoption signal; installers could prioritize aligned households or design tools to move oppositional ones.

insights.claim: Even 'in sync' households often required an external catalyst such as an expiring tax credit or escalating bills to actually move to solar adoption.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Timing sales pushes to tax-credit expirations and bill spikes is the conversion lever - directly actionable for solar demand forecasting and policy-driven demand pull-forward.
insights.quote: catalysts (for example, expiring tax credits, escalating bills) were often necessary to move in-sync households to adoption.

insights.claim: In a survey of 394 lost and current adopters, romantic partners and parents in adopter households played larger roles, executed more tasks, initiated more discussions, and expressed greater positivity than in lost-customer households.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Multi-member engagement distinguishes converts from drop-offs; measuring household participation could improve solar lead-scoring and reduce customer-acquisition cost.

insights.claim: Greater household-wide support and even disagreement predicted solar adoption, indicating that active engagement - not consensus alone - drives the decision.
insights.speaker: Nature Energy
insights.so_what: Counterintuitively, disagreement signals engagement and predicts adoption; marketers should provoke discussion, not just seek the agreeable buyer.
insights.quote: Greater household-wide support and even disagreement predicted adoption.
